title: Lesson 10: Repentance and the Learning Circle
description: Everyone drifts. Whether through obvious rebellion or quiet self-righteousness, we all wander off the narrow road Jesus talked about. This lesson uses the story of the prodigal son and an interactive tool called The Learning Circle to walk you through the biblical rhythm of repentance and belief, showing that the Father is not waiting to punish but waiting to welcome you home.

blocks.text: You have walked nine lessons now, learning to hear Jesus, obey Him, rest in Him, and grow roots. But real growth is not straight lines. We stumble. We wander off His narrow path. Like the prodigal son who bolted for freedom and landed in the mud, or his older brother who stayed home but hardened in pride. Both sons ditched the Father. Both forgot His heart. And both needed to turn back.

This lesson is not about shame. It is about grace. When sin pulls you away, whether into rebellion or self-reliance, God does not wait for you to crawl back. He is already running. And the rhythm of turning back to Him is something every follower of Jesus needs to learn, not just once, but over and over again.
The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe in the gospel.

When Jesus began His public ministry, He announced it in two words: repent and believe. Two actions. One movement. You cannot have one without the other. This is the heartbeat of the Christian life, and it never stops beating. Martin Luther began his 95 Theses with this: "When our Lord and Master Jesus Christ said 'repent,' he willed the entire life of believers to be one of repentance." He was not saying you get saved over and over again. He was saying that this ongoing turning, away from trusting yourself and toward trusting God, is the posture of the whole Christian life.

blocks.title: The Prodigal's Return
blocks.text:
Picture the younger son. He asked for his inheritance early, which in that culture was the same as saying, "I wish you were dead." He left home, blew everything, and woke up starving among pigs. He did not come back perfect. He came back broken. But the Father did not lecture. He did not wait for an apology or for the son to clean himself up. He ran.

"But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him." (Luke 15:20)

In that culture a dignified man never ran, but this father did. The robe, the ring, the fattened calf: extravagant restoration, all given before the son finished his rehearsed speech. That is the heart of God the Father toward you. He is not waiting for you to get your life together. He is running toward you right now.

Then there is the older brother. He never left the yard, but pride kept him outside the celebration. He stayed home, kept all the rules, did everything right, and yet when the party started he was furious. He thought he was fine until he realized he was just as lost, only in a different ditch. He had never received the father's love because he had been managing his performance record instead of being a son. Both sons forgot something simple: the Father's heart is wide open. He is not waiting to punish. He is waiting to welcome.

10.1 - Which son do you relate to more right now: the one who ran away into obvious sin, or the one who stayed home but hardened in pride and self-righteousness? What does that tell you about where you are with the Father?
The Learning Circle

So what do you do when you realize you have drifted? Whether you bolted like the younger son or stiffened like the older one, the gospel calls you home. The tool below is called The Learning Circle, adapted from the work of 3DM Ministries. It is a simple, biblical rhythm that helps you turn around when you have wandered off the path. Everyone drifts. When it happens, you have a choice: keep going like nothing ever happened, or stop and turn back. The Learning Circle guides you through both steps of that turn.
Tap "Begin" and walk through each step slowly. Notice what it feels like to follow the path from drifting off to coming home.
Walking Through the Circle

First, you notice the drift. You are walking the path of Jesus, and then you veer off. Maybe it is anger that flares up, a lie you told, a habit you cannot shake, or a slow slide into self-reliance where you stop asking God for anything because you think you have it handled. Like the prodigal packing his bags, you are simply off the path. No drama, no guilt trip. Just honesty. Name it.
All we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned, every one, to his own way; and the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all.

Then you hit the turning point and enter the circle. The right side is Repent. This is where you pause, reflect, and let the heart feel the weight. Reflect means you look honestly at what happened. God shines a light on the why, maybe a weakness you ignored, a lie you bought into, a pattern you have been running from. The Psalmist says God desires truth in our inner parts. Ask yourself: where did I miss His voice? What pulled me away? Then comes remorse, not beating yourself up but letting the ache do its work, because you were made for more than this. Like the son in the pigpen realizing, "This is not who I am." Conviction hurts, but it is love waking you up.
For godly grief produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret, whereas worldly grief produces death.

The left side of the circle is Believe. This is where repentance gets practical. First you plan. Jesus said, "Go and sin no more," but He never meant alone. Think small and real: who can keep you accountable? What one change can you make? What is your first move tomorrow? Then you obey. You walk it out. Humility shows up when you confess, when you change, when you trust that He is stronger than your mess. You do not earn grace. You just receive it and let it move your feet.
If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.

Finally, you return to the path. Not at the same spot, but further along. The prodigal did not just get home; he learned the Father was not stingy but lavish. You grow too. You see where pride was hiding. You see where truth sets you free. Each turn through the circle brings you closer to the Father's heart, not because you performed well, but because humility cracked you open and let His grace pour in.
Rejoice not over me, O my enemy; when I fall, I shall rise; when I sit in darkness, the Lord will be a light to me.

10.2 - As you walked through the Learning Circle, which step felt most personal to you right now? Is there something specific God is asking you to reflect on, feel the weight of, plan around, or obey?

10.3 - Think about the difference between worldly grief (feeling bad because of consequences) and godly grief (feeling the weight because you were made for more). Where do you see each one showing up in your own life right now?
10.4 - The "Believe" side of the circle asks you to plan and obey. What is one specific, practical step you can take this week to walk differently? Who could you ask to hold you accountable?
The Father at the Gate

As you finish this tenth lesson, our first full circle around repentance, remember: the prodigal did not come home perfect. He came home broken, hungry, unsure. But that is exactly why the Father ran. Not to scold, but to cover him, feed him, restore him. Every time you drift off the path, into sin's ditch or pride's, God is not shocked. He is ready. The Learning Circle is not about fixing yourself. It is about turning back so He can show you who He really is: kind, patient, lavishing grace on the weak.
Or do you presume on the riches of his kindness and forbearance and patience, not knowing that God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance?

You learn His love deeper here, not because you earned it, but because humility cracked you open. His kindness is what draws you back. Your failures do not disqualify you. They just prove you need Him more. So do not fear the drift. Fear staying there. Because each return makes you stronger, not in willpower, but in knowing you are loved anyway.
Growing Up from Here

And now, as this first course wraps up, you are not starting over. You are stepping forward. These ten lessons in Growing Deep have been about the vertical relationship between you and God: hearing His voice, learning to love Him, understanding what He did for you at the cross, and now learning to turn back when you wander. You have tasted repentance as gift, not grind. You have seen that the Father's arms are open, always.

The next ten lessons in Course 202, Growing Up, build on everything you have planted here. You will learn what it looks like to live this out in community, in mission, and in the rhythms of everyday life. The roots go deeper as the branches grow wider. Keep turning. Keep believing. You have got this, because He has got you.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
